name: Doc Cheatham
description: American jazz trumpeter, singer, and bandleader (1905-1997)
abstract: Adolphus Anthony Cheatham, better known as Doc Cheatham (June 13, 1905 – June 2, 1997), was an American jazz trumpeter, singer, and bandleader. He is also the grandfather of musician Theo Croker.

interviews.title: Doc Cheatham interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 28, 1995

quotes.text: Doc Cheatham’s remarkable career in jazz reads like a fairy tale. Born Aldolphus Cheatham in 1905, he grew up in Nashville and took up the sax and trumpet at an early age. His first playing experience was at the Bijou Theater where he played behind Bessie Smith, later recording on the soprano sax with another blues great, Ma Rainey. His search for further playing opportunities took him to Chicago where he came under the spell of Louis Armstrong, eventually playing beside him and substituting when Satchmo was double booked. He devoted himself completely to the trumpet and became a sought- after lead player, performing with the big bands of Cab Calloway, Teddy Wilson, Benny Carter and Chick Webb. The demise of the big bands resulted in a new career for Doc, as he became a trumpet star with the Latin bands of Machito and Tito Puente. At the age of 60 he became the trumpet soloist with the Benny Goodman Sextet and later appeared with the Basie Alumni Band. He has been featured at Carnegie Hall with Wynton Marsalis and was the subject of a recent Nightline interview with fellow jazz veterans Milt Hinton and Lionel Hampton. Doc can be heard in New York every Sunday at Sweet Basil’s where he plays the history of jazz through his horn.
quotes.caption: General biography mentioning a feature at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Doc Cheatham interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 28, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Keith Ingham
about_quotes.evidence: But I was lucky enough to play with some of the greats of the era that I particularly love, like Vic Dickinson and Doc Cheatham, both now gone.
about_quotes.doc_title: Keith Ingham interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Jon-Erik Kellso
about_quotes.evidence: And I got to play with Doc Cheatham a couple of times... So that was 'The New McKinney's Cottonpickers' featuring Doc Cheatham
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon-Erik Kellso interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Howard Alden
about_quotes.evidence: shortly after I moved to New York I made a recording with Doc Cheatham... I’d be traveling around the world with him
about_quotes.doc_title: Howard Alden interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Nick Brignola
about_quotes.evidence: I played with Vic Dickinson and Doc Cheatham, I played, you know Dixieland with these guys, I worked gigs with them.
about_quotes.doc_title: Nick Brignola interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, July 28, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: George Wein
about_quotes.evidence: I played with Buck Clayton and Vic Dickinson and Doc Cheatham and Buddy Tate
about_quotes.doc_title: George Wein interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Carter Benny
about_quotes.evidence: I had a job at the Empire Ballroom with my orchestra, and I managed to get Doc Cheatham to play with me in that orchestra.
about_quotes.doc_title: Carter Benny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
